messages.content: isn't it wild how a coffee that was like 2 bucks when i was a teenager is basically 6 now

Yeah, it really is striking when you think about it that way! There's something about a specific everyday purchase like coffee that makes inflation feel really *real* and personal, compared to just hearing abstract percentages in the news.

Do you remember roughly when you were a teenager? Because the timing matters a lot - if that was like 20-30 years ago, some of that price increase is just normal inflation over time. But honestly coffee has also gotten more expensive *faster* than general inflation in recent years, especially with supply chain issues and bad harvests in coffee-growing regions.

And then there's also the whole specialty coffee culture shift - like even a basic drip coffee at a lot of places has kind of gotten pulled upmarket in how it's priced.

Does it change your habits at all, or do you just kind of absorb the cost?
i was telling my coworker, feels like everything just quietly crept up and we all kinda accepted it

That's such a good observation - "quietly crept up" is exactly the right way to put it. It's like the boiling frog thing, where each individual price increase feels small enough to just shrug off, but then you look back and realize how much things have shifted.

And there's probably something psychological going on too - like once a price becomes the new normal, it's hard to even remember being outraged about it. You just... adjust.

It's kind of interesting that you and your coworker were talking about it though - do you find that it comes up a lot lately? Because I feel like people are more openly frustrated about cost of living stuff than they used to be. It used to almost feel impolite to complain about prices, like you were being cheap or something. Now it seems more like a shared common experience people bond over.

The tricky part is that wages have also gone up for a lot of people, so it's not always a straightforward "we're worse off" story - but it definitely *feels* that way for a lot of everyday purchases, especially food and drinks.
makes me a little nostalgic for the days when a 20 in your pocket felt like real money lol

Haha right?! A $20 used to feel like you were set for the day - grab some food, maybe a coffee, catch a movie, and still have change left over. Now it's like... that's basically one decent lunch.

There's something kind of bittersweet about that nostalgia too, because it's not just about the money. It's tied up with a whole feeling of that time in your life - like the simplicity of it, fewer responsibilities, that feeling of freedom a $20 could buy you as a teenager.

It's funny how money can be such a strong memory trigger like that. A lot of people have really specific anchors - like "a candy bar was 50 cents" or "gas was under a dollar" - and those little price memories almost become a way of marking time and how much the world has changed.

Do you find yourself actually more nostalgic for that era in general lately, or is it more just a passing "huh, weird how things change" kind of feeling? Because I think a lot of people are in a reflective mood about that stuff right now.
anyway not complaining really, just one of those shower thoughts about how prices change over time

Ha, the best conversations start as shower thoughts honestly! There's something about that kind of idle musing that's just... pleasant. Not really a problem to solve, just an interesting thing to notice about the world.

And it's lunchtime now - maybe go enjoy that $6 coffee anyway 😄
